title: Module 2 quiz: DNS, SSL, and securing a domain

questions.prompt: Your team orange-clouds the mail subdomain. Email stops working immediately. Why?

choices.text: Cloudflare proxies HTTP/HTTPS only — not SMTP or other mail protocols. Mail records (MX, mail subdomain) must stay gray-cloud / DNS-only. Always.

questions.explanation: Standard Cloudflare proxy is HTTP/HTTPS-only. Mail and any non-HTTP service must stay gray-cloud.

questions.prompt: Your origin is on Vercel (CNAME-only). You want `example.com` to point at it. What's the right move on Cloudflare?

choices.text: Manually maintain A records pointing at Vercel's IPs.
Use CNAME on the apex with Cloudflare's CNAME flattening. Cloudflare resolves the CNAME at query time. Standard DNS prohibits apex CNAME; Cloudflare's flattening makes it work seamlessly.

questions.explanation: CNAME flattening solves the 'can't CNAME on apex' problem for hosted services with rotating IPs.

questions.prompt: What's the only correct Cloudflare SSL mode for production?

choices.text: Full (strict): TLS on both legs with cert validation at origin. Other modes either skip encryption on Cloudflare↔origin (Flexible — insecure) or skip validation (Full — accepts self-signed).

questions.explanation: Full (strict) + Cloudflare Origin Cert = end-to-end TLS with no renewal hassle. The canonical production setup.

questions.prompt: An attacker bypassed your Cloudflare WAF and DDoS'd your origin directly. How did they find the origin IP?

choices.text: Most likely via leak: mail server on same IP, old DNS history, CT logs, or subdomain pointing direct. Mitigation: split mail to different IP, lock origin firewall to Cloudflare IP ranges, audit for leaks periodically.

questions.explanation: Origin IP leaks are common via mail records, CT logs, old DNS. Firewall lockdown to Cloudflare IPs is the structural defense — even with leaks, attackers can't connect.

questions.prompt: Behind Cloudflare, your rate limiter reads `request.remoteAddress` and treats all traffic as one user. Fix?

choices.text: Read the real client IP from the `cf-connecting-ip` header (or properly parse `X-Forwarded-For`). Configure your framework's trust-proxy settings. The connection's remote IP behind Cloudflare is the Cloudflare edge, not the user.

questions.explanation: Cloudflare passes the real client IP in headers. Your app must read those for rate limiting, logging, analytics. Default 'remote IP' reads see the edge IP and apply limits app-wide — exactly wrong.
